content:
# Joshua Chapter 17 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 240-241 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_017_page_001.jpg`

---

*[Manasseh's Allotment — Eighteen verses of tribal territory and tribal complaint. The chapter opens by honoring Machir, Manasseh's firstborn and a man of war, then turns to the unprecedented case of Zelophehad's daughters, who claimed their inheritance before Eleazar the priest. The boundary descriptions give way to the chapter's true drama: Manasseh cannot drive out the Canaanites, and the house of Joseph comes to Joshua demanding more land. Joshua's reply is magnificent — equal parts encouragement and challenge. "You are a numerous people with great strength. You will not have just one allotment. The hill country will be yours — clear it! And the Canaanites, even with their iron chariots — you will drive them out." The chapter is bookended by the tension between divine promise and human responsibility.]*

---

**1** This was the allotment for the tribe of Manasseh, because he was Joseph's firstborn. As for Machir, Manasseh's firstborn, the father of Gilead — because he was a man of war, Gilead and Bashan were given to him. **2** And allotments were made for the rest of the descendants of Manasseh, by their clans: for the descendants of Abiezer, the descendants of Helek, the descendants of Asriel, the descendants of Shechem, the descendants of Hepher, and the descendants of Shemida. These were the male descendants of Manasseh son of Joseph, by their clans.

**3** Now Zelophehad son of Hepher, son of Gilead, son of Machir, son of Manasseh, had no sons — only daughters. These were the names of his daughters: Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah. **4** They came before Eleazar the priest, before Joshua son of Nun, and before the leaders, and said, "YHWH commanded Moses to give us an inheritance among our kinsmen." So, in keeping with YHWH's command, he gave them an inheritance among their father's brothers. **5** Manasseh's share came to ten portions, besides the land of Gilead and Bashan that lay beyond the Jordan, **6** because the daughters of Manasseh received an inheritance among his sons. The land of Gilead belonged to the rest of Manasseh's descendants.

**7** The boundary of Manasseh ran from Asher to Michmethath, which faces Shechem. Then the boundary went southward to the inhabitants of En-Tappuah. **8** The land of Tappuah belonged to Manasseh, but Tappuah itself, on Manasseh's border, belonged to the descendants of Ephraim. **9** Then the boundary went down to the Wadi of Kanah. South of the wadi, these towns belonged to Ephraim among Manasseh's towns. Manasseh's boundary ran along the north side of the wadi and came out at the sea. **10** The land to the south was Ephraim's, and the land to the north was Manasseh's, with the sea as its boundary. They touched Asher on the north and Issachar on the east.

**11** Within Issachar and Asher, Manasseh held Beth-Shean and its surrounding towns, Ibleam and its surrounding towns, the inhabitants of Dor and its surrounding towns, the inhabitants of En-Dor and its surrounding towns, the inhabitants of Taanach and its surrounding towns, and the inhabitants of Megiddo and its surrounding towns — the three of Naphath. **12** But the descendants of Manasseh could not take possession of these towns, and the Canaanites were determined to stay in that land. **13** When the Sons of Israel grew stronger, they put the Canaanites to forced labor, but they did not drive them out completely.

**14** The descendants of Joseph spoke to Joshua: "Why have you given us only one allotment and one portion as our inheritance? We are a numerous people, and YHWH has blessed us greatly until now." **15** Joshua said to them, "If you are such a numerous people, go up into the forest and clear land for yourselves there, in the territory of the Perizzites and the Rephaim — since the hill country of Ephraim is too cramped for you."

**16** The descendants of Joseph said, "The hill country is not enough for us, and all the Canaanites living in the valley land have iron chariots — both those in Beth-Shean and its surrounding towns and those in the Valley of Jezreel." **17** Joshua said to the house of Joseph — to Ephraim and Manasseh — "You are a numerous people with great strength. You will not have just one allotment. **18** The hill country will be yours. Yes, it is forest — but you will clear it, and its farthest reaches will be yours. You will drive out the Canaanites, even though they have iron chariots and even though they are strong."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v. 1 — "man of war" (אִישׁ מִלְחָמָה):** The Hebrew explains *why* Machir received the Transjordan territories — Gilead and Bashan were frontier regions requiring military strength. The assignment matches the man.

**v. 3-4 — Zelophehad's daughters:** This passage directly echoes Numbers 27:1-11, where these five women made legal history by petitioning Moses for inheritance rights in the absence of male heirs. YHWH ruled in their favor. Here they invoke that ruling before Joshua, and it holds. The Hebrew verb form ("he gave them") is singular — Joshua personally honors the precedent.

**v. 5 — "ten portions" (עֲשָׂרָה חֲבָלִים):** The math: Manasseh had six clans (v. 2), but Hepher's line produced five daughters instead of one male heir. Five daughters plus five remaining male-line clans equals ten portions. The text is quietly showing that the daughters' inheritance expanded, not diminished, the tribe's total allotment.

**v. 9 — Wadi of Kanah (נַחַל קָנָה):** "Reed Wadi" — a seasonal streambed marking the boundary between Ephraim and Manasseh. The complicated boundary description in vv. 7-10 reflects a real-world situation where the two Joseph tribes' territories were interleaved, with Ephraimite towns embedded within Manasseh's borders.

**v. 11 — "the three of Naphath" (שְׁלֹשֶׁת הַנָּפֶת):** One of the most debated phrases in Joshua. The Hebrew *naphath* likely means "heights" or "hill-districts." The "three" may refer to the three major elevated regions (Dor, Taanach-Megiddo, Beth-Shean) within the broader lowland territory. PNEUMA retains the Hebrew term since no English equivalent captures it cleanly.

**v. 12-13 — Failure and compromise:** The Hebrew verb הוֹאִיל ("was determined, insisted") describes the Canaanites' resolve to remain. Verse 13 reveals a recurring pattern in Judges-era Israel: growing strong enough to dominate but settling for forced labor (מַס) instead of displacement. This half-measure becomes a theological problem throughout the book.

**v. 14-18 — The complaint and Joshua's challenge:** The descendants of Joseph essentially say: "We're too big for one lot, and the valley is too dangerous." Joshua's response is masterful. He does not give them more land — he gives them a harder task. The word כִּי ("because/for/yes") in v. 17 is affirmational — Joshua is not consoling them but challenging them. "You are great — so *act* like it." The iron chariots (רֶכֶב בַּרְזֶל) represent the peak of Canaanite military technology, yet Joshua insists they can be overcome.

**v. 15 — "Rephaim" (רְפָאִים):** An ancient population associated with extraordinary physical size, often mentioned alongside the Anakim. Their presence in the forest hill country explains why it remained unsettled — and why Joshua's challenge carried real weight.

**v. 18 — "its farthest reaches" (תֹּצְאֹתָיו):** Literally "its goings-out" — the outermost edges of the territory. Joshua promises them not just the hill country's center but everything to its borders, provided they have the courage to take it. The chapter ends on this note of conditional promise — the land is theirs if they will fight for it.
